trajectory_summary: The call moves from a warm, engaged open to a booked appointment in under 12 minutes — a clean structural result. Parker executes the verify section efficiently, handles the billing complication practically, and gets the calendar slot. The energy, however, is flat throughout: Parker never anchors the bill emotionally, never flips the utility cost against Leland's stated pain, and the button-up is partial and out of sequence. The appointment is real but thin — Leland's resilience carried the call more than Parker's technique.
outcome_dispute: The call ends with a booked appointment for 9AM the following day. The v1 outcome tag is unknown, but the appointment is real. The booking is conditional on the bill photo being sent (Parker has not received it by call end) and the wife's inclusion was noted but not addressed in the appointment structure — she is on the title but not confirmed for the sit. The appointment is fragile on two dimensions: bill not yet received, and co-decision-maker not included.

primary_coaching_focus.why: Leland confirms his wife is on the title at 02:11 and she is never mentioned again. The appointment is booked for Leland alone. Per protocols.spouse_decision, a co-title-holder must be included in the appointment — this is a procedure, not an objection to overcome. The closer walks into a sit where the co-decision-maker may not be present, which is the structural vulnerability the schema explicitly names as the failure mode. This is the highest-priority coaching beat because it directly threatens the sit's validity and Parker's compensation.

key_moments.stewart_read: Leland confirms his wife is on the title. Parker acknowledges ('Okay. Gotcha.') and moves on. The wife is never mentioned again — she is not included in the appointment, not asked about her availability, and not confirmed as a participant in the closer call. The appointment is booked for Leland alone. Strategic hypothesis: Parker intended to address spouse inclusion at button-up and forgot. Reactive hypothesis: Parker treated this as a data point rather than a protocol trigger. The reactive read holds — there is no later move that addresses the wife's inclusion.

key_moments.quote: Well, yeah, definitely see if we can do that. You know, at least cut that back for you, make it a little bit more stable and steady for you, save you some money there.
key_moments.stewart_read: Parker captures the bill range ($100 low, $200s high) and responds with a generic savings promise. The schema's capture-and-flip pattern requires the bill to be turned into emotional alignment against the utility — Parker does the capture and drops the flip entirely. This is the universal floor failure mode the schema names explicitly.

key_moments.quote: I just got a bill for $584. And I wonder what — so I think that's probably — they're back charging me. Yeah. Because amount due, $1,039.26.
key_moments.stewart_read: Leland discovers a $1,039 back-charge in real time on the call. This is a financial vulnerability disclosure — the schema's explicit category. Parker's response is a navigation prompt ('do you see, like, a usage history or a KWH usage or anything like that?'). The $1,039 figure receives zero acknowledgment. This is the call's highest-leverage empathy moment and the rep walks straight past it. Note: Leland is resilient — he self-recovers at 10:53 ('I would've been shocked, you know, if I didn't know what this was about'), which means the appointment survived. But the trust cost is real and the closer inherits it.

key_moments.quote: Oh, yeah. I would've been shocked, you know, if I didn't know what this was about.
key_moments.stewart_read: Leland self-recovers from the billing anomaly and contextualizes the September 5,104 kWh spike with calm self-awareness. This is an enthusiasm signal: a customer who can articulate his own usage patterns, who has been tracking his bill closely enough to notice the discrepancy, and who is not rattled by the complexity. He is a high-engagement, high-retention prospect. Parker responds with 'Oh, wow. Okay. Gotcha. Yeah. That's a big jump there.' — functional but flat. The moment was an opening to anchor the utility's unreliability as the exact problem solar solves.
